definition: Costs that do not change with short‑term output (e.g., founder salary, core software subscriptions, office rent).
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Determines burn rate floor and runway; even with zero revenue these costs must be covered, so they cap how long the experiment can run.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: List all recurring monthly expenses (tools, infra, minimal compensation) and sum them to a monthly figure; cross‑check against invoices and bank statements when available.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: For very early, a lean software startup often targets fixed costs under 10–20k per month for a 2–3 person team in developed markets.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use to compute burn and runway; penalise founders whose fixed costs are high relative to expected pace of learning or milestone scope.

definition: Incremental cost directly tied to one additional unit of output (e.g., cloud usage per active user, payment processing fee, per‑shipment logistics).
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Shapes gross margin and scaling potential; low variable cost gives more operating leverage once revenue appears.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Identify unit (user, order, API call). Estimate direct costs per unit from vendor price sheets, cloud calculators, or pilot data; refine as usage data arrives.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: For many software products, target variable cost under 20–30% of revenue per unit to allow healthy gross margins later.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use to approximate gross margin bands (high/medium/low) and to favour models that can scale without linear cost growth.

definition: Total cost of serving one additional unit of demand, often similar to variable cost in early software startups.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Indicates scalability; near‑zero marginal cost suggests strong economics once product‑market fit is found.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Combine direct variable cost (cloud, payments) plus any labour that scales with volume (support per active cohort); divide incremental cost by incremental units.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: Digital products often approach marginal cost near zero after infrastructure is in place; physical or services businesses tend to have higher marginal cost.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Tag businesses with structurally low marginal cost as higher upside within the portfolio, all else equal.

definition: Current monthly or quarterly revenue extrapolated to an annualised figure.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Even tiny real revenue is a powerful signal of willingness to pay and execution; differentiates projects from businesses.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: If there is any recurring revenue, multiply latest month by 12 to get ARR; for non‑recurring, annualise based on last 3–6 months’ average.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: At pre‑seed, many funds treat even 1–5k monthly recurring revenue as meaningful traction if customer quality is good.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use as a soft uplift to the Market/Traction axis, not a hard requirement; do not penalise zero revenue in clearly pre‑revenue categories.

definition: How clearly the founder can explain who pays, for what, and on what cadence (subscription, usage, one‑off, marketplace take rate).
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: A clear, testable revenue model reduces go‑to‑market risk even before dollars are booked.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Ask for a one‑sentence revenue model description and a simple example invoice; stress‑test with 2–3 pricing scenarios in a lightweight spreadsheet.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: Simple, standard models (SaaS, usage‑based, transactional) are usually lower risk than complex multi‑sided pricing for first‑time founders.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Score higher when the revenue model is standard, easy to test with small pilots, and aligned with how the target customer already buys.

definition: Revenue per unit minus direct variable costs associated with that unit, before fixed costs.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Shows whether each incremental unit is directionally value‑creating, even if the company is loss‑making overall at small scale.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: For the main unit (seat, workspace, shipment), estimate average price and subtract payment fees, hosting cost, and any per‑unit labour.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: Healthy software businesses often target 70–80% gross margin over time; early pilots may be lower.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use rough contribution margin bands (negative/low/medium/high) as a risk flag; avoid models that only work with unrealistic scale assumptions.

definition: Average cost to acquire a paying customer, including paid marketing and sales effort.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Determines how efficiently capital turns into demand; high CAC with low lifetime value is a red flag for scalability.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: For early founders, use pilot experiments: total spend on campaigns or outreach hours valued at a simple hourly rate, divided by number of converted customers.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: In many SaaS and consumer internet categories, a payback period under 12–18 months on CAC is considered healthy once the model matures.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use as directional only at 0‑investment stage; treat extremely high CAC in pilots as evidence to push for model or channel changes before scaling.

definition: Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortisation; proxy for operating profit.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Shows whether the current plan is structurally high‑burn or can approach break‑even at modest scale; informs how much runway 100k meaningfully buys.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: For very early teams, model a simple 12‑month P&L using estimated revenue, fixed costs, and variable costs; compute EBITDA as revenue minus operating expenses.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: Most venture‑backed companies are expected to be EBITDA‑negative at seed; the question is whether burn is buying validated learning and durable assets.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use scenario analysis (base/bull/bear) to see how far a 100k check moves EBITDA towards break‑even under realistic assumptions.

definition: Bottom‑line profit after all expenses, interest, and taxes.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: At 0‑investment stage this is almost always negative; the main value is in understanding how losses scale with activity and what break‑even might look like.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Use the same rough P&L as for EBITDA but subtract any financing costs if present; taxes can usually be ignored until profitability is plausible.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: New ventures are typically loss‑making for several years; the key is that the path to net profit is plausible and not purely dependent on unrealistic pricing or volume.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Treat persistent, structurally unfixable negative net margin models as low‑priority for allocation, especially if capital intensity is high.

definition: Net cash outflow per month (cash expenses minus cash inflows).
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Determines how long the company can survive and experiment before needing more capital; crucial for small, fast checks.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Sum all expected monthly cash expenses and subtract realistic cash inflows (if any); do not include non‑cash items.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: For a 100k check, many early‑stage investors prefer burn that provides 12–18 months of runway to reach the next meaningful milestone.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use burn to check whether the requested allocation is sized appropriately for the stated milestone plan; penalise plans that consume capital in under 6–9 months without clear inflection.

definition: Number of months the company can operate at current or planned burn before cash is exhausted.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Links check size directly to time for learning and execution; too little runway increases financing and shutdown risk.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Divide available cash (existing plus proposed investment) by estimated monthly burn; create base and worst‑case variants.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: A common target is 12–18 months of runway per round for seed‑stage companies, with some preferring slightly shorter but more frequent top‑ups.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use as a hard constraint in allocation rules: e.g., avoid allocations that yield under 9 months runway unless upside is exceptional and follow‑on capital is very likely.

definition: Qualitative measure of how many concrete learning or traction milestones a team can hit with a fixed amount of capital.
why_it_matters_for_new_founder: Critical for founders with no track record: it indicates discipline and planning, even without historical financials.
how_to_estimate_with_no_financials: Ask founders to list 3–5 specific milestones (e.g., ship beta, close 10 pilots, reach 1k MAU) and estimate cost and time for each; cross‑check against burn and runway estimates.
typical_benchmark_or_rule_of_thumb: Well‑planned early teams often map 100k to at least one major learning milestone and one early traction milestone.
recommended_use_in_allocation_model: Use as a qualitative score feeding into the Idea‑vs‑Market and Founder axes: prioritise teams who can clearly articulate milestone‑per‑dollar thinking.
